policy_statement: Pilot geothermal/marine heating in Heka buildings and expand its use based on experiences.
mechanism_description: Heka will pilot geothermal/marine heating in buildings and expand its use based on experience, replacing fossil-based heating with low-carbon thermal energy and reducing building emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Building heat supply change (geothermal/marine heat) → ↓ fossil fuel combustion for heating → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Buildings (or their connected heat networks) switch heating provision from fossil boilers/CHP/district fossil heat to geothermal/sea-water heat systems (often via heat pumps and thermal exchange).  
(2) Mediating process: Renewable/low-carbon heat extraction and heat-pump amplification reduces or eliminates combustion; remaining energy input is primarily electricity, with emissions dependent on grid intensity, typically lower than direct fossil heat.  
(3) Outcome: Lower CO₂ (and other combustion-related GHG) emissions per unit of delivered heat, decarbonizing building heat demand.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced greenhouse gas emissions from building space/water heating by replacing fossil-based heat with geothermal/marine-sourced low-carbon thermal energy.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: replace fossil-based building heating with geothermal/marine low-carbon heat, leading directly to lower combustion emissions.  
2) Why Mitigation over alternatives: the primary intended and largest climate effect is emissions reduction, not reduced energy use per se (Resource Efficiency) and not reducing hazard exposure (Adaptation); any efficiency gains are secondary to fuel switching.  
3) Edge cases applied: if electricity for heat pumps is very carbon-intensive, net mitigation could be reduced, but the mechanism remains primarily aimed at heat decarbonization; if framed as resilience (e.g., energy security), that is ancillary.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: any policy implementing geothermal/marine heating deployment for buildings shares the same dominant effect—displacing fossil heat and reducing GHG emissions.  
5) Secondary subtype assignment (e.g., “electrification/heat pumps”, “district energy decarbonization”, “renewable heat”) is deferred to policy-level evidence and implementation details.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the system is primarily a heat-pump electrification project on a high-emitting grid or relies on fossil backup/peaking, realized GHG reductions may vary; nevertheless, the mechanism’s dominant pathway remains heating decarbonization via low-carbon thermal supply.
key_indicators: "will pilot the use of geothermal/marine heating"; "expand the use thereof based on the experiences"
co_benefits: Improved local air quality (reduced combustion); potential energy cost stability; reduced exposure to fossil fuel price volatility
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as a planning/strategy commitment (pilot then scale), but the core action is deployment of heating systems in buildings, which fits infrastructure investment best.
